coredata.dc:description: Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a new treatment method being developed against traditional cancer therapy methods. In this study, graphene oxide-iron oxide-polypyrrole (GO-Fe3O4-PPy) and iron oxide@polypyrrole-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs) ternary nanocomposites having superior photothermal and magnetic properties were loaded with doxorubicin (DOX) anticancer drug. The loading and release of the DOX was investigated by UV–visible spectroscopy. At the end of 48 h, the cumulative release of DOX from GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4 was obtained as 99.55% ± 0.20, 48.87% ± 0.20% and 97.88% ± 0.45, 38.82% ± 0.26, respectively. After a short period of time like 4 h and 8 h, NIR-triggered release of DOX was increased significantly compared to drug release without NIR. The cytotoxic effects on the 4T1 mouse breast cancer cells of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX drug loaded nanocomposites were determined by MTT test. The increase of DOX release with NIR effect was demonstrated by both in-vitro cytotoxicity studies and confocal laser microscopy images. In summary, multifunctional GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites have great potential for photothermal chemotherapy.

head.ce:title: An anticancer drug loading and release study to ternary GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites for photothermal chemotherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a new treatment method being developed against traditional cancer therapy methods. In this study, graphene oxide-iron oxide-polypyrrole (GO-Fe3O4-PPy) and iron oxide@polypyrrole-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs) ternary nanocomposites having superior photothermal and magnetic properties were loaded with doxorubicin (DOX) anticancer drug. The loading and release of the DOX was investigated by UV–visible spectroscopy. At the end of 48 h, the cumulative release of DOX from GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4 was obtained as 99.55% ± 0.20, 48.87% ± 0.20% and 97.88% ± 0.45, 38.82% ± 0.26, respectively. After a short period of time like 4 h and 8 h, NIR-triggered release of DOX was increased significantly compared to drug release without NIR. The cytotoxic effects on the 4T1 mouse breast cancer cells of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX drug loaded nanocomposites were determined by MTT test. The increase of DOX release with NIR effect was demonstrated by both in-vitro cytotoxicity studies and confocal laser microscopy images. In summary, multifunctional GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites have great potential for photothermal chemotherapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a common disease in the world that affects different parts of the body. According to the 2020 report of the World Health Organization (WHO), 18 million new cancer cases were seen in 2018 and 10 million of the deaths occurred were caused by cancer. By 2040, it is predicted that there will be 29–37 million cancer cases [1]. Traditional methods such as surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy and biological therapy are widely applied in cancer therapy. However, these methods lead to limited therapeutic efficiency and serious side effects. To overcome the drawbacks of the conventional methods, photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) methods are being developed [2–5].

Photothermal therapy (PTT) is the local destruction of cancer cells by converting the light energy into heat energy. In this treatment method, NIR (near infrared radiation) laser in the wavelength range of 700–1100 nm is generally preferred as the beam source. NIR uses an optically absorbing photothermal agent that can convert light energy into heat energy [6–8].

Photothermal chemotherapy, one of multiple treatment methods, contains both drug and heat effects has a synergistic effect to destroy cancer cells. The NIR effect leads to triggered and controlled drug release from nanomaterials [9–11]. Multi-photothermal agents are needed for photothermal chemotherapy with great potential in cancer therapy. Graphene oxide (GO) [12], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [13], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [14], silica [15], gold [16], Fe3O4 [17], polyaniline (PANI) [18], polypyrrole (PPy) [19] are among the materials used for photothermal chemotherapy.

GO and GQDs belong to family of graphene known as the thinnest 2D material. GO has many oxygen based functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy) leading to increase solubility and easy surface functionalization [20,21]. GQDs, a zero-dimensional carbon material have high biocompatibility and photostability, excellent optical property, good solubility and photoluminescence properties. GQDs can be doped with nitrogen (N) atoms commonly and NGQDs with more active regions can be used in bioimaging, photocatalysis, drug and gene delivery [22–25]. A conductive polymer PPy has high absorbance in the NIR region and exhibits excellent biocompatibility and photostability [26,27]. In addition, Fe3O4 which is among the magnetic nanostructures with superparamagnetic properties is preferred for applications such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), targeted drug release and hyperthermia [28,29].

In the literature, there are some multifunctional magnetic photothermal agents including PPy or GQDs, besides that doxorubicin (DOX) is widely studied as a model chemotherapy drug. Cheng et al. (2021) synthesized magnetic Fe3O4/g-C3N4 @PPy-DOX nanocomposite for chemotherapy/PDT/PTT of cancer [30]. To determine photothermal properties of Fe3O4/g-C3N4 @PPy nanocomposite, different concentrations of dispersions (0.1–0.3 mg/mL, 1 W/cm2) and different power density of laser (0.5 W/cm2 – 1 W/cm2, 0.2 mg/mL) were applied for 10 min under 638 nm irradiation. DOX release was achieved as 20% after 78 h and 72% after 75 h at pH 7.4 and pH 6.5, respectively. Multifunctional HNT@Fe3O4 @PPy@DOX nanocomposite containing halloysite nanotubes was synthesized as a photothermal agent by Luo et al., 2020 [31]. The temperature of the drug loaded nanocomposite increased by ∼21 °C under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.2 W/cm2 and 1 mg/mL). The cumulative release of DOX was found as about 60% and 5% with or without laser irradiation, respectively. Yang et al. (2018) loaded DOX on the Fe3O4 @MnO2 @PPy nanocomposite for magnetic-targeting-assisted chemotherapy and enhanced PDT/PTT [32]. The photothermal conversion performance of nanostructures were investigated, however the drug release from Fe3O4 @MnO2 @PPy nanocomposite was realized without NIR effect. After 65 h, 26.8%, 67.3% and nearly 100% of DOX released at pH 7.4, pH 6.5 and pH 6.5 + H2O2, respectively. Magnetic mesoporous silica (Fe3O4/SiO2) graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposite (MMSN/GQDs) was synthesized as photothermal agent by Yao et al. (2017) [33]. The effective temperature rise of nanocomposite was detected by using a 808 nm diode laser with a power of 2.5 W/cm2. The cumulative DOX release from magnetic nanocomposite was investigated at different temperature (37 °C, 50 °C) and pH (5.0, 7.4) values. At pH 5.0 and 50 °C conditions, the highest cumulative release percentage was achieved as about 50% after 10 h. Wang et al. (2013) investigated the drug release from Fe3O4 @PPy-PEG-DOX nanocomposite with or without NIR effect [34]. Within 24 h, about 48%, 13% and 4% of DOX released without NIR at pH 5.0, 6.0 and 7.4, respectively. NIR-triggered release of DOX exhibited higher percentages especially at acidic conditions.

In this work, the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded on graphene oxide-iron oxide-polypyrrole (GO-Fe3O4-PPy) and iron oxide@polypyrrole-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs) with superior properties and high photothermal performance. Drug release studies from nanocomposites carried out with or without NIR (808 nm) effect. Besides, in-vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake studies of drug loaded nanocomposites were realized on 4T1 cells. According to our research, all these studies were achieved for the first time in the literature.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, 98.0–102.0%, Sigma-Aldrich) and phosphate buffered saline (PBS, Sigma-Aldrich) were used for drug loading and release studies on GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites. The materials used in the cell culture studies with 4T1 mouse breast cancer cells (ATCC®) were RPMI-1640 medium (ATCC®), fetal bovine serum (FBS, Sigma-Aldrich), penicillin-streptomycin (PS, Sigma-Aldrich), trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25%, Sigma-Aldrich), Dulbecco’s phosphate buffered saline (DPBS, Sigma-Aldrich), 3-(4,5-dimethythiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, Sigma-Aldrich), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich), paraformaldehyde powder (95.0%, Sigma-Aldrich) and bisBenzimide H 33342 trihydrochloride (Hoechst 33342, for fluorescence ≥ 97.0%, Sigma-Aldrich).

The ternary GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites were synthesized as stated in our previous studies [35,36]. To obtain anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) loaded ternary nanocomposites, at first, 10 mg/mL nanocomposites aqueous dispersion prepared via ultrasonication at approximately 4 °C for 10 min. Afterwards, 10 μL of the nanocomposite solution was mixed with 990 μL PBS solution (pH 10) and 100 μL of 1 mg/mL DOX solution. After mixing in an orbital shaker overnight at 150 rpm and room temperature, DOX loaded nanocomposites (GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX) were obtained by centrifugation at 14,500 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was separated to calculate DOX loading capacity and encapsulation efficiency by Eqs. (1, 2) via UV–vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of 480 nm. The drug-loaded nanocomposites were freeze-dried for 24 h and stored at 4 °C [37–39].

The drug loaded and unloaded ternary GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit BioTwin), scanning electron microscopy (QUANTA 400 F Field Emission SEM), UV–vis spectroscopy (Shimadzu-UV 1601, Japan) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Shimadzu FTIR 8400-S, Japan) with spectra in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements was performed with a PHI 5000 VersaProbe for characterization of surface elemental chemical composition of NGQD, GO, Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs and GO-Fe3O4-PPy.

The photothermal properties of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites in PBS solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL were determined by NIR laser (Newport, 808 nm) at different laser power densities (1.5–2.5 W/cm2). The temperature differences were recorded with a thermal camera (FLIR E50) during 10 min irradiation and 20 min of cooling. PBS solution was used as the control group. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of the drug loaded ternary nanocomposites given in Eq. (3) was calculated according to the literature [35,36].

To release of the DOX without NIR effect, 1 mL of PBS medium (pH 7.4 and pH 5.5) was added to GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites and the release studies were performed in an orbital shaker at 37 °C and 130 rpm. At a certain intervals (0.25–48 h), the suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15–45 s and additional magnetic separation was also performed to prevent any nanocomposite lost during separation of the supernatant. The separated PBS medium was kept for analysis and an equal volume of fresh PBS medium was added to the nanocomposites. For the drug release under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation, DOX loaded GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites in PBS solution (1 mL) were irradiated with power density of 2.0 W/cm2 for 10 min during a certain time intervals (0.25 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h (and 8 h)) and replenished with an equal volume of fresh PBS medium. The amount of released DOX for each release study was determined using a UV–vis spectroscopy at the wavelength of 480 nm. All of the measurements were performed in triplicate.

In-vitro cytotoxicity assays were realized using 4T1 mouse breast cancer cells (ATCC) by MTT test. Cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin streptomycin (PS). 4T1 cells were seeded in 96-well cell culture plates (5 ×104 cells per well) and incubated at 37 °C in a CO2 (5%) incubator (Thermo Scientific) for 24 h. At the end of 24 h, the culture media were replaced with the media containing the experimental groups. The experimental groups were GO-Fe3O4-PPy, GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX, Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs, Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX and free DOX. TCPS was used as control. Free DOX was applied at a concentration of either 5 or 20 µg/mL. Accordingly, the concentrations of the nanocomposites were adjusted to contain either 5 or 20 µg/mL of DOX. The experiments were performed with or without NIR effect. After 4 h incubation, irradiation was applied with a NIR laser (2 W/cm2) at a wavelength of 808 nm for 3 min. The culture was continued for 24 h. Subsequently, the media were removed and the cells were washed with DPBS. For MTT analysis, 150 μL fresh culture medium and 50 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) were added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. To dissolve the formazan crystals, DMSO (150 μL) was added to each well and the absorbance values were determined by a microplate reader (Rayto, RT2100C) at 570 nm.

Cellular uptake of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites was investigated with and without NIR effect. 4T1 cells were cultivated in glass bottom cell culture dishes (1 ×105 cells per dish) at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Afterwards, the culture media were replaced with fresh media containing GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX or Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites. The concentrations of the nanocomposites were adjusted to contain 20 µg/mL of DOX. After 2 h of incubation, the cells were irradiated by an 808 nm NIR laser at a power density 2 W/cm2 for 3 min. At the end of 4 h, the media were removed, the cells were washed with DPBS and fixed in 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde. Finally, the cells were stained with Hoechst 33342 and visualized using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Carl Zeiss LSM 880). The excitation/emission wavelengths for Hoechst 33342 and DOX were 405/500 nm and 488/604 nm, respectively.

All data were expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was carried out by unpaired t-test using the GraphPad Prism program version 8.4.3 (686). P values of less than 0.05 indicated statistical significance.

FE-SEM image of GO-Fe3O4-PPy nanocomposite, which was synthesized via in situ polymerization of pyrrole on GO-Fe3O4 surface in methanol-water media, was given in Fig. 1. The twisted and curved edges of the GO nanosheets were clearly visible in addition, the surface of the GO nanosheets was covered with Fe3O4 nanoparticles and PPy polymeric structure was in the nested form with a large particle size (20–45 nm) than Fe3O4 nanoparticles (4–8 nm).

TEM image of GO-Fe3O4-PPy and HRTEM image of Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite were given in Fig. 2A and B, respectively. It was determined that the binary GO-Fe3O4 structure synthesized by the co-precipitation approach was effectively, than coated with larger particle size PPy nanospheres efficiently (Fig. 2A), consistent with the SEM images (Fig. 1) [35]. According to Fig. 2B, it was concluded that NGQDs nanostructures with a spherical structure with an average size of 4 nm were covered the core@shell Fe3O4 @PPy structure via the self-assembly method. Additionally, the quantum efficiency of the ternary Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs structure was determined as 6.5% (inset, Fig. 2B) as found also our previous study [36]. As a result, the ternary GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite structures were successfully synthesized for use in DOX loading studies.

The surface elemental chemical composition of Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs and GO-Fe3O4-PPy nanocomposites were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), in addition, XPS survey spectra of GO and NGQDs were also provided for comparison ( Fig. 3). Fig. 3A shows the survey spectra of NGQDs and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite. The spectrum of NGQDs displays three major components at 283, 398 and 529 eV corresponding to the signals of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, showing that graphene quantum dots effectively doped with nitrogen, which was reported also in our previous study [36]. In the Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite spectrum, besides signals of C1s, N1s and O1s, some new peaks emerged at 167, 231 and 711 eV and were assigned to S2p, S2s and Fe2p, respectively [40–42]. Results from XPS spectral curves show that the atomic contents of C, O, N, S and Fe in Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposite are 65.9%, 22.9%, 8.0%, 1.7% and 1.6%. The signal of Fe2p originates from Fe3O4 nanoparticles, whereas the appearance of S2p and S2s further confirms that the stabilizing agent DBSA is well interacted with the nanocomposite surface [41,43,44]. Similarly, the survey spectra of GO and GO-Fe3O4-PPy nanocomposite were given in Fig. 3B. The spectrum of GO displays two major components which correspond to the signals of C1s and O1s, that is the 285 and 532 eV, respectively [45]. In the GO-Fe3O4-PPy nanocomposite spectrum, the new peaks at the binding energies of 167, 231, 398 and 711 eV could be assigned to S2p, S2s, N1s and Fe2p, respectively [40–42]. Furthermore, % atomic concentrations of C, O, N, S and Fe are 73.6, 16.9, 7.2, 2.0 and 0.3, respectively. The N signal in the GO-Fe3O4-PPy spectrum is attributed to the N-C linkages in the polypyrrole rings [46]. The signal of Fe for both Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs and GO-Fe3O4-PPy nanocomposite samples is not strong. It was not easily detected because Fe3O4 core is covered by the PPy shell and the depth of the XPS test materials is only in a few nanometers [31,44]. These data show that both nanocomposites were successfully synthesized which is in agreement with the above mentioned TEM results (Fig. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading studies and characterization of drug loaded nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Drug loading capacity (%) and encapsulation efficiency (%) of the DOX were calculated for GO-Fe3O4-PPy ternary nanocomposite as 45.44 ± 0.37% and 83.28 ± 1.25% and for Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposite as 46.68 ± 0.20% and 87.56 ± 0.72%, respectively. In this study, DOX was loaded on GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposites for the first time. DOX loading capacity (%) and encapsulation efficiency (%) obtained in this study were superior values compared to the literature including PPy or GQDs nanostructures [47–49].

UV–vis spectra of the commercial DOX, ternary nanocomposites with or without anticancer drug were shown in Fig. 4. The characteristic peak of the DOX is located at 480 nm [50,51]. In the spectra of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX (Fig. 4A) and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX (Fig. 4B), DOX peaks were seen in addition to characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 (330–450 nm) and PPy (∼ 460 nm, > 700 nm) [52–54].

Fig. 5 showed that FTIR spectra of the GO-Fe3O4-PPy, GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX (Fig. 5A), Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX (Fig. 5B) nanocomposites. The spectra of the nanostructures exhibited the characteristic peaks of the Fe3O4, PPy and NGQDs structures. However, the peaks of GO were not seen due to effective coating by Fe3O4 and PPy [35,36]. The spectra of the drug loaded nanocomposites included the characteristic peaks of the DOX C–H stretching at 2924 cm−1, CO stretching (ketone) at 1720 cm−1, CO stretching at 1620 cm−1and C–O stretching (primary alcohol) at 972 cm−1 [55,56]. UV–vis and FTIR results confirmed that DOX had been successfully loaded on the ternary nanocomposites. Besides, FE-SEM images indicated that there is no physical degradation after drug loading on the nanocomposites ( Fig. 6).

In Fig. 7, temperature change profiles and photothermal images under 808 nm NIR effect of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX solutions in PBS were given. The temperature of the GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX solution (0.1 mg/mL, in PBS) increased to 45.6 °C, 56.5 °C and 66.1 °C (Fig. 7A) at 1.5 W/cm2, 2 W/cm2, 2.5 W/cm2 power densities, respectively. Similarly, 808 nm NIR effect was applied on the Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX solution with 0.1 mg/mL concentration during 10 min and the temperatures of 42.8 °C, 47.8 °C ve 58.3 °C (Fig. 7C) were reached end of the duration with regard to different power densities. Besides that, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) values were given in Table 1. η values were calculated between 33.4% and 38.4% for GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and 28.4–31.5% for Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX. Both η and Tmax values increased by increasing power density. ΔTmax values obtained after 10 min NIR effect were found higher than 5 min irradiation.

According to these results, drug release studies with NIR were carried out at moderate power density (2 W/cm2) for 10 min under 808 nm NIR irradiation. Cancer cells can be killed by keeping them at 42 °C for 15–60 min or at temperatures above 50 °C for 4–6 min [57]. The high Tmax and η values obtained under NIR effect showed that DOX loaded GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs can be used for photothermal chemotherapy applications.

The cumulative release profiles of DOX from GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites were given in Fig. 8. After 48 h, cumulative drug release without NIR from GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX were found as 99.55 ± 0.20% and 48.87 ± 0.20% at pH 5.5 and 7.4 (Fig. 8A), respectively. When 2 W/cm2 NIR irradiation was applied, the cumulative release values from GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX reached to 99.69 ± 0.80% at pH 5.5 and 47.75 ± 0.20% at pH 7.4 after 4 h. NIR irradiation increased the cumulative release values at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4 by 27.36% and 16.33% (Fig. 8B), respectively. DOX release from Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites was calculated as 97.88 ± 0.45% (pH 5.5) and 38.82 ± 0.26% (pH 7.4) (Fig. 8C). Similarly, NIR effect increased the cumulative drug release from Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX. At the end of 8 h, it was found that the values as 95.87 ± 1.42% (pH 5.5) and 44.26 ± 0.71% (pH 7.4). The cumulative drug release values with NIR effect enhanced 16.82% for pH 5.5% and 15.38% for pH 7.4 compared to values without NIR effect (Fig. 8D).

As expected, the cumulative release values were higher at acidic pH (5.5) for both ternary nanocomposites. At acidic pH, the protonation of the amino group in the DOX molecule in other words the formation of conjugated acid facilitates the release of the drug from the nanocomposites [58]. In our study, two different ternary nanocomposites with superior potential for photothermal chemotherapy applications were used. The release of DOX loaded on the GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs ternary nanocomposites for the first time was investigated at different pH, with or without NIR effect. When the results are compared with the similar studies in the literature, it was found that better cumulative release percentages were obtained seen in Table 2. Consequently, high DOX release percentages in a short time were achieved from the ternary nanocomposites (GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX, Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX) with high drug content and encapsulation efficiency.
Fig. 9 shows the results of in-vitro cytotoxicity of the free DOX and nanocomposites containing 20 µg/mL of DOX. No effective results have been obtained at 5 µg/mL of DOX (Data not shown).

Without NIR effect, the cell viability for GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites was obtained as 82.45% (•••P < 0.001) and 73.73% (•••P < 0.001), respectively. Similarly, GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs caused to 77.75% (••P < 0.01) and 70.75% (•••P < 0.001) cell viability under 808 nm NIR effect. There was no statistical difference between the viabilities of the cells treated with the nanocomposites with or without NIR effect, where very low toxicity was observed [30,61,62]. The drug loaded ternary nanocomposites were expected to have at least as much cytotoxic effect as free DOX. Nevertheless, free DOX exhibited more effective cytotoxicity than GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX (*P < 0.05) nanocomposites without NIR irradiation. Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites showed better cytotoxicity compared to GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites (ΔΔΔP < 0.001) and had almost as much cytotoxic effect as free DOX (ns, P ≥ 0.05) on 4T1 cancer cells. The cytotoxicity of free DOX was higher than GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites (***P < 0.001) with NIR. However, Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX showed the most effective cytotoxicity on 4T1 cells compared to free DOX (***P < 0.001) and GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites (ΔΔΔΔP < 0.0001) in the presence of NIR. In the stationary cell culture media, GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites were agglomerated, whereas Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites were dispersed more homogeneously. As a result, the drug release from GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites was limited during in-vitro cell culture studies. This situation reduced the cytotoxic effect of this ternary nanocomposites compared to Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites. Over 95% cumulative drug release was achieved from both ternary nanocomposites at pH 5.5 in PBS medium and in a shaker (Fig. 8) since the agglomeration was prevented in the shaking medium. The cell viability decreased from 43.56%, 65.27% and 44.67–39.97%, 54.69% and 29.3% with NIR irradiation for free DOX, GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX, respectively. Since the NIR effect triggered drug release from nanocomposites, the cytotoxic effect of both nanocomposites was increased under NIR irradiation (cell viability during GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX treatment without NIR compared to GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX with NIR, □□P < 0.01; Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX treatment without NIR compared to Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX with NIR, ✚✚✚✚P < 0.0001). As a result, Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX exhibited higher cytotoxicity than free DOX and GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX on 4T1 mouse breast cancer cells with controlled drug release and NIR irradiation.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis was realized to observe the cellular uptake of drug loaded GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites into 4T1 cells. The cells were localized by staining the cell nucleus with Hoechst 33342. The same region of cells was monitored for both DOX and Hoechst 33342 and the CLSM images were given in
Figs. 10 and 11. DOX and Hoechst 33342 had red and blue fluorescence, respectively. The merged images clearly revealed that both GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites were uptaken by the cells and DOX was released into the cells. The increased red fluorescence signals of DOX indicated the increased drug release under NIR irradiation. CLSM images of Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites (Fig. 11) exhibited increased fluorescence signals of DOX than GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites (Fig. 10). This result was compatible with the results of in-vitro cell culture studies (Fig. 9) and confirmed less drug release of GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX than Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs/DOX nanocomposites due to the agglomeration of the GO-Fe3O4-PPy/DOX nanocomposites in the cell culture medium.

In conclusion, the anticancer drug DOX was loaded for the first time onto GO-Fe3O4-PPy and Fe3O4 @PPy-NGQDs nanocomposites with superparamagnetic and high photothermal efficiency properties. The increase of drug release with 808 nm NIR irradiation had also been proven by cell culture studies. This study is promising in the future for the use of ternary magnetic nanocomposites containing PPy, Fe3O4 and GO/NGQDs with superior properties in photothermal chemotherapy treatments compared to most of the similar studies using different nanocomposites in terms of photothermal efficiency and drug release.
